sourceMovie.title: Gintama: The Movie
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Gintama: The Movie (2010) stuck because it commits to its action premise without apologizing—viewers remember set pieces and momentum as much as plot. Shinji Takamatsu's control of tone keeps the ride coherent. The action and science fiction blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Resident Evil: Degeneration
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Gintama: The Movie clicked for you, Resident Evil: Degeneration (2008) is a strong next stop: both trade in action and science fiction, with unease that lingers after the credits. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

recommendations.title: Batman Ninja
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Gintama: The Movie clicked for you, Batman Ninja (2018) is a strong next stop: both trade in action and science fiction, with earned warmth without mush. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

faq.question: What should I watch after Gintama: The Movie?
faq.answer: Try Resident Evil: Degeneration, Batman Ninja, Batman: Gotham Knight—each shares DNA with Gintama: The Movie while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Gintama: The Movie?
faq.answer: Gintama: The Movie (2010) is best described as Action, Science Fiction, Animation. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Gintama: The Movie part of a franchise?
faq.answer: Yes—it's part of the "Gintama Collection" storyline. You can enjoy it standalone, but sequels and tie-ins build on characters and continuity established here.
